title: Hormuz crisis could lead to constructive dialogue between ASEAN and China in the South China Sea
gist: The Hormuz-driven oil shortage forced the Philippines into a China 'reset' and joint South China Sea oil exploration talks, creating an unexpected but fragile opening for President Marcos to trade energy cooperation for a long-sought South China Sea Code of Conduct — a deal Trump could also claim as a diplomatic win.

themes.theme: Philippine energy crisis forces a China reset

insights.claim: The Philippines imports 98 per cent of its oil from the Gulf, and the Hormuz-driven choking of global oil trade caused acute fuel shortages severe enough for the government to declare a national emergency.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Illustrates how a Middle East chokepoint crisis can force otherwise-adversarial Asian powers into unexpected cooperation — a spillover mechanism worth tracking for other oil-import-dependent states.
insights.locator: Perhaps most of all in the Philippines

insights.claim: With inflation at 7.2 per cent in April and an angry population facing fuel shortages, President Ferdinand Marcos Jr stated he was looking to 'reset' relations with China and reopen conversations on joint oil and gas exploration in the disputed South China Sea, despite the two countries' significant territorial tensions; Beijing welcomed the move and talks were held immediately.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Domestic economic pain from an external energy shock is directly reshaping Philippine-China diplomacy — a leading indicator that energy security crises can accelerate geopolitical realignments faster than years of prior diplomacy.
insights.locator: But faced with an angry population

insights.claim: The Philippines has made clear that any deeper agreement with China would only follow tangible Chinese commitments to the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) and the signing of a South China Sea Code of Conduct (CoC).
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Manila is using its leverage from the energy crisis to sequence any China economic opening behind a binding legal/security commitment — a negotiating template worth watching for replication elsewhere.
insights.locator: The Philippines is open to further talks

themes.theme: Hormuz vs. South China Sea: stakes comparison

insights.claim: A blockade of the Strait of Hormuz threw global markets into chaos because 20 per cent of the world's oil supply passes through it; by contrast, 60 per cent of all global trade runs through the South China Sea, meaning disruption there would be far more consequential.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The South China Sea's far larger share of global trade throughput makes its stability a bigger tail risk to the global economy than Hormuz, even though Hormuz has dominated recent headlines — a reason to weight South China Sea risk more heavily in scenario planning.
insights.quote: By contrast, 60 per cent of all global trade runs through the South China Sea.
insights.locator: A blockade in the Strait of Hormuz threw global markets into chaos

themes.theme: ASEAN summit and Code of Conduct momentum

insights.claim: The Philippines, as current ASEAN chair, hosted a shortened, 'bare-bones' 48th ASEAN summit in Cebu due to the regional energy crisis, at which all 11 ASEAN nations focused on energy and food security through regional integration while adopting an ASEAN Leaders' Declaration on Maritime Cooperation founding a new maritime security centre in the Philippines to enforce UNCLOS and freedom of navigation.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The new Philippine-based maritime centre is a concrete institutional outcome to track as a bellwether for how seriously ASEAN follows through on South China Sea maritime governance.
insights.locator: Last week the Philippines hosted the 48th ASEAN summit

insights.claim: A Chinese foreign ministry spokesperson signaled appetite for a CoC deal after the ASEAN summit, encouraging ASEAN to 'resist disruption' to a deal that would allow parties to 'better manage differences, advance cooperation, and keep the South China Sea peaceful and stable.'
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Explicit Chinese rhetorical openness to a CoC, paired with Philippine conditions, suggests the negotiating window is more live now than in recent years.
insights.quote: better manage differences, advance cooperation, and keep the South China Sea peaceful and stable
insights.locator: Appetite for a CoC agreement also seems high in Beijing

insights.claim: Joint oil exploration as an incentive for a CoC deal aligns with policy values China has held since Deng Xiaoping, who encouraged joint development and shared economic interests as a route to peacefully settling territorial disputes; under such a deal China would gain access to billions in South China Sea oil and gas reserves without violating international law or drawing US and allied ire, while also saving the significant resources it currently spends encroaching on disputed waters.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: China has a genuine economic and resource-cost incentive to accept a CoC now, not just a diplomatic one — a reason this window may be more durable than prior failed CoC talks.
insights.locator: Joint oil exploration in the South China Sea is a valuable incentive

themes.theme: US role and Trump's dealmaking incentive

insights.claim: The US has made significant investments in Philippine military infrastructure over the past four years and ran its largest-ever joint military drill with Manila last month, reaffirming its commitment to Philippine territorial sovereignty and positioning Washington to help ensure implementation of any CoC.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Deepening US-Philippine military ties give Washington both leverage and incentive to back a CoC, making US buy-in a more plausible near-term outcome than in past CoC efforts.
insights.locator: Over the past four years, the US has made significant investments

insights.claim: Facilitating a South China Sea Code of Conduct between China and ASEAN would be a diplomatic win — 'a jewel in his cap' — for President Trump given his self-proclaimed talent for brokering peace deals.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A CoC brokered partly through Trump's personal dealmaking brand could become a headline foreign-policy achievement, giving the US an added incentive to actively push the process forward rather than remain passive.
insights.locator: With President Trump’s self-proclaimed talent for brokering peace

insights.claim: President Marcos has indicated a CoC must come before any other economic conversations with China, but analysts have urged caution about entangling interests in disputed waters, and the 'perennial stumbling block' for this opening — as with past South China Sea diplomacy — will be follow-through on the details.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Treat the current opening as a real but fragile negotiating window; the base rate for South China Sea diplomatic follow-through has historically been poor, so implementation risk should temper optimism.
insights.locator: The perennial stumbling block will be follow-through
